schools.summary: Chrysostom: as a man in forming a body places the head first, then the neck, then the feet, so Paul proceeds by gradual advances — having spoken of the husband (who has authority over the wife) and the wife (second in authority), he comes now to the third rank, that of children, over whom both the husband and the wife have authority; Paul charging children to obey their parents in the Lord, for this is right — the children's obedience as a thing right in itself (Paul laying on children the duty of obedience to their parents, 'in the Lord,' in all things agreeable to the Lord's will and as part of their obedience to him, grounding the command not merely on parental authority but on the plain rightness of the thing: that children should obey those who gave them life is just and fitting in the very order of nature and of God).

themes: children, obey your parents in the Lord
for this is right
the children's duty of obedience
right in itself, in the order of nature and of God

schools.summary: Gill: this explains who parents are and points at branches of obedience due to them; they are not only to be loved, feared, and reverenced, their corrections submitted to, offences against them acknowledged, their tempers borne with and infirmities covered, but they are to be honoured in thought, word, and gesture — highly thought of, spoken to and of with reverence; Paul citing the command to honour father and mother, noting it is the first commandment with a promise attached — the honour due to parents sealed by a promise (Paul grounding the children's obedience in the fifth commandment of the Decalogue, which requires not bare obedience but honour, reverence, esteem, submission, and care toward both parents, and marking it 'the first commandment with promise,' the first precept to which a particular reward is annexed, God himself crowning the honouring of parents with a special blessing).

themes: honour thy father and mother
which is the first commandment with promise
the honour due to parents
crowned by God with a special promise

schools.summary: Gill: the promise is that it may be well with the child, in this world and that which is to come; Barnes: the whole commandment as recorded in Deuteronomy adds 'that thy days may be prolonged, and that it may go well with thee in the land which the Lord thy God giveth thee'; Paul stating the promise — that it may be well with the child and he may live long on the earth — the promise of well-being and long life (Paul giving the very promise attached to the fifth commandment: that the child who honours his parents shall fare well and enjoy length of days, a promise holding generally, the honouring of parents tending, in God's ordinary providence, to a prosperous and lengthened life, and which the gospel raises to its higher fulfilment in the well-being and eternal life of the obedient).

themes: that it may be well with thee
thou mayest live long on the earth
the promise of well-being and long life
honouring parents tending to a blessed life

schools.summary: Gill: fathers provoke their children to wrath neither by words (unjust and unreasonable commands, contumelious and reproachful language, frequent and public chidings, indiscreet and passionate expressions) nor by deeds (preferring one to another, denying them the necessaries of life, not allowing proper recreation, severe and cruel blows, inhuman usage); Paul charging fathers not to provoke their children to wrath but to bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord — the balance of parental authority with loving discipline (Paul, having required children's obedience, fencing the parents' authority against abuse: not to exasperate their children by harshness, injustice, or unreasonable severity, which provokes resentment and discouragement, but to rear them with godly discipline, 'nurture,' and instruction, 'admonition,' in the Lord's ways, training them up for God).

themes: ye fathers, provoke not your children to wrath
bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord
the balance of authority with loving discipline
rearing children in the Lord's ways

schools.summary: Chrysostom: not husband only, nor wife, nor children, but virtuous servants also contribute to the organization and protection of a house, so Paul has not overlooked this department, coming to it last because it is last in dignity and rank, yet addressing them in a far more advanced tone than the children; Paul charging servants to obey their masters according to the flesh (their earthly masters, not their lords in spiritual things) with fear and trembling, in singleness of heart, as unto Christ — the service rendered to masters as service to Christ (Paul laying on servants the duty of hearty obedience, with reverent care, fear and trembling, sincere and undivided purpose, singleness of heart, and above all 'as unto Christ,' so the lowliest service, done as to the Lord himself, is dignified and made an act of worship).

themes: servants, be obedient to your masters according to the flesh
with fear and trembling, in singleness of your heart
as unto Christ
the lowliest service made worship

schools.summary: Gill: eyeservice is doing work only when under the master's eye, then pretending great diligence and industry to ingratiate oneself into his affections, but neglecting his business when he is absent; whereas servants ought to attend his service in his absence as well as his presence, and so seek to please him, which is commendable; Paul charging servants to serve not with eyeservice as menpleasers, but as servants of Christ doing the will of God from the heart — the sincere service that works for God, not appearance (Paul forbidding the false, eye-pleasing service that labours only when watched and only to win favour, and requiring instead a true, inward service: working as Christ's own servants, whose eye is always on them, doing the will of God from the heart, with the same faithfulness whether the earthly master sees or not).

themes: not with eyeservice, as menpleasers
but as the servants of Christ
doing the will of God from the heart
sincere service that works for God

schools.summary: Gill: service is to be done to masters with good will — not grudgingly, with an ill will, no otherwise nor longer than when forced to it, but of a ready mind and with a cheerful spirit, taking delight in the work and reckoning it a pleasure to serve, as an Israelite not sold does his work with his good will and according to his own mind; Paul charging servants to do service with good will, as to the Lord and not to men — the willing, glad service offered ultimately to Christ (Paul calling for service rendered not reluctantly or as a hated necessity but with a glad and ready heart, and lifting the whole transaction above the earthly relation: the servant regarding his work as done 'to the Lord, and not to men,' so even bond-service becomes a willing offering to Christ, the true Master being served).

themes: with good will doing service
as to the Lord, and not to men
cheerful, willing service
even bond-service a glad offering to Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the good thing is done according to the will of God, from right principles in the heart, and with a view to God's glory; Barnes: whatever a man does that is right, for that he shall be appropriately rewarded, no matter what his rank in life, if he discharges his duty to God and man; Paul assuring servants that whatever good thing any man does, the same he shall receive of the Lord, whether bond or free — the impartial reward of the Lord for faithful service (Paul crowning his charge to servants with the cheering certainty that the Lord, the great Master, marks and rewards every good deed, and that without respect to a man's earthly station, so the lowliest bond-servant, faithful in his work, shall receive of Christ the same reward as the free, his humble service not overlooked but recompensed).

themes: whatsoever good thing any man doeth, the same shall he receive of the Lord
whether he be bond or free
the impartial reward of the Lord
the lowliest service recompensed

schools.summary: Gill: 'do the same things' refers not to service and obedience but to singleness of heart, benevolence, humanity, and a regard to Christ and the will of God, and the doing of their duty as they would have their servants do theirs (the duty of religious masters being, with respect to their servants' souls, to instruct them and use them to religious things); Paul charging masters to do the same things to their servants, forbearing threatening, knowing their own Master is in heaven, with whom is no respect of persons — the masters' equity grounded in their accountability to God (Paul laying on masters the duty to treat servants with the same conscientious goodwill, sparing harsh threats and cruelty, and to remember they themselves have a Master in heaven before whom master and servant stand alike, for God shows no respect of persons, judging great and lowly by the same impartial standard).

themes: ye masters, do the same things unto them, forbearing threatening
knowing that your Master also is in heaven
neither is there respect of persons with him
the masters' equity grounded in accountability to God

schools.summary: Gill: this is the conclusion of Paul's exhortations, in which he addresses the saints as his brethren — not merely as a familiar way of speaking among the Jews but in regard to them as regenerate persons of the same family and household of God with himself, to show his humility and as a proof of his affection; Paul calling the brethren to be strong in the Lord and in the power of his might — the call to draw strength from the Lord alone (Paul, as he turns to the great theme of spiritual warfare, sounding the keynote: the believer is not to trust his own strength, which is no match for his enemies, but to be strong 'in the Lord, and in the power of his might,' drawing all his courage and ability for the battle from union with Christ and the inexhaustible store of God's own mighty power).

themes: finally, my brethren, be strong in the Lord
and in the power of his might
the call to draw strength from the Lord alone
not his own strength, but God's might

schools.summary: Gill: the armour is not that which God himself is sometimes clothed with and uses against his enemies, but what he has provided for his people and furnishes them with, the particulars of which follow; it is called 'the armour of God' because prepared by him for his people and bestowed on them by him, and because it is in its own nature divine and spiritual, not carnal; Paul commanding them to put on the whole armour of God, that they may stand against the wiles of the devil — the full divine armour against the devil's craft (Paul calling the believer to be clad not in part but in the whole, complete, armour God supplies, every piece needful, so that, fully equipped with God's own provision, he may stand his ground against the devil, not his open force only but his 'wiles,' the cunning, crafty stratagems by which the enemy seeks to deceive and overthrow).

themes: put on the whole armour of God
that ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil
the full divine armour
against the devil's crafty stratagems

schools.summary: Gill: this is a reason why saints should be strong in the Lord and put on the whole armour and prepare for battle, since their enemies are not 'flesh and blood' (frail mortal men, such as were wrestled against in the Olympic games) but spiritual foes; Paul declaring that the believer's wrestling is not against flesh and blood but against principalities, powers, the rulers of this world's darkness, and spiritual wickedness in high places — the foe a host of mighty evil spirits (the real enemies not human but a vast and organized army of evil spirits: the fallen angels in their ranks and orders, principalities and powers, the dark rulers of this present age, hosts of spiritual wickedness in the heavenly places, mighty, malignant, and unseen, against whom no fleshly strength can prevail).

themes: we wrestle not against flesh and blood
against principalities, against powers
the rulers of the darkness of this world... spiritual wickedness in high places
the foe a host of mighty evil spirits

schools.summary: Gill: this is a repetition of the exhortation, made necessary by the many powerful enemies mentioned in the preceding verse, serving to explain what is meant by putting on the armour and leading to the account of its several parts; Barnes: 'the evil day' is the day of temptation and trial; Paul repeating the command to take the whole armour of God, that they may withstand in the evil day and, having done all, stand — the full equipment for the day of trial and final standing (Paul, in view of such mighty foes, urging again the taking up of God's complete armour, with a double aim: that the believer may 'withstand,' resist and hold out, in the evil day, the season of fierce temptation and assault, and that, when the whole conflict is over and all is done, he may still be found standing, unfallen and victorious).

themes: take unto you the whole armour of God
that ye may be able to withstand in the evil day
having done all, to stand
the full equipment for the day of trial

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having drawn up this army and roused their zeal (both that they should be arrayed and subject to each other and that their spirit should be aroused) and inspired them with courage, Paul now proceeds to arm them — for arms would have been of no use had they not first been posted each in his own place and the spirit of the soldier aroused; Paul calling them to stand, with loins girt with truth and the breastplate of righteousness — the girdle of truth and the breastplate of righteousness (the believer's loins girt with 'truth,' the truth of the gospel held fast and sincerity and integrity of heart, which braces and binds up the whole man for action like the soldier's belt, and his heart guarded by the 'breastplate of righteousness,' the righteousness of Christ for justification and a holy life, covering the vital part against the enemy's thrusts).

themes: stand therefore
having your loins girt about with truth
the breastplate of righteousness
truth bracing the man, righteousness guarding the heart

schools.summary: Gill: the gospel is called the gospel of peace because it makes men of peaceable tempers and behaviour, gives peace to distressed minds, directs the way to eternal peace, and publishes the peace made by the blood of Christ (with a better claim to the name than the law); Paul directing that their feet be shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace — the gospel of peace as the soldier's footing (Paul giving the believer shoes for his feet: the firm footing and readiness the gospel of peace supplies, so that, as the soldier needs sure footing to stand and march without slipping or being wounded, the believer, established in the gospel which proclaims peace with God through Christ's blood and ready to walk and witness in it, has steady ground beneath him for the whole campaign).

themes: your feet shod
the preparation of the gospel of peace
the gospel as the soldier's footing
firm footing and readiness for the campaign

schools.summary: Gill: the shield of faith may be understood either of the grace of faith (like a golden shield, precious, solid, and substantial, like the shield of mighty men by which mighty things are done, by which the believer not only repels but conquers the enemy) or rather of the object of faith, Christ, which faith lays hold of; Paul calling them, above all, to take the shield of faith, by which they can quench all the fiery darts of the wicked one — faith the shield that extinguishes Satan's darts (Paul giving faith a place above the rest, the shield that covers the whole man and is moved to meet every assault: by faith, laying hold of Christ and God's promises, the believer catches and quenches the 'fiery darts' of the wicked one, the flaming arrows of temptation, doubt, blasphemy, and despair, so they fall harmless and burnt out).

themes: above all, taking the shield of faith
wherewith ye shall be able to quench all the fiery darts of the wicked
faith the shield that extinguishes Satan's darts
the flaming arrows of temptation and doubt quenched

schools.summary: Gill: the helmet of salvation may mean either Christ himself the Saviour (the Arabic rendering 'the helmet of the Saviour'), or the salvation he is the author of and a well-grounded hope of it, which is a defence of the head against false doctrines as a helmet is armour for the head and an erecter of the head; Paul directing them to take the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God — the helmet of hope and the offensive sword of Scripture (Paul naming the last two pieces: the 'helmet of salvation,' the assured hope of salvation in Christ, which lifts up the head and guards the mind against discouragement and error, and the one weapon of attack, 'the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God,' the Scripture wielded in the Spirit's power, the keen blade by which the believer both wards off and strikes down the enemy, as Christ repelled the tempter with 'it is written').

themes: take the helmet of salvation
the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God
the helmet of hope, the sword of Scripture
the word of God the one weapon of attack

schools.summary: Gill: the last weapon is prayer, taking in all sorts — mental and vocal, public and private — and every branch of it (deprecation of evils, petition for good things, thanksgiving for mercies), used always, opposed to those who pray not at all, or have left it off, or pray only in distress; Paul calling them to pray always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit, watching thereunto with perseverance and supplication for all saints — the all-pervading practice of watchful, persevering prayer (Paul, having named the armour, adding the spirit in which it is all worn and the warfare waged: constant, varied prayer offered 'in the Spirit,' by his help and influence, kept up with alert watchfulness and unwearied perseverance, and reaching out in intercession not for self alone but for all the saints, the whole army together).

themes: praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit
watching thereunto with all perseverance
supplication for all saints
prayer the atmosphere of the whole conflict

schools.summary: Gill: this shows the apostle's great humility and his sense of the greatness of the work of the ministry, that it is the duty of people to pray for their ministers, that no man is perfect in this life, and that the more superior members need the assistance of the inferior ones (the request made not in dissimulation or feigned humility but in sincerity); Paul asking that they pray for him, that utterance be given him to open his mouth boldly and make known the mystery of the gospel — the apostle's humble plea for utterance and boldness (Paul, who has just commanded prayer for all the saints, asking a place in it for himself, requesting not relief from his bonds but two gifts for his ministry: 'utterance,' the right words given of God, and 'boldness,' freedom and courage to speak, that even in prison he might make known the gospel-mystery without fear).

themes: and for me, that utterance may be given unto me
that I may open my mouth boldly
to make known the mystery of the gospel
the apostle's humble plea for utterance and boldness

schools.summary: Gill: the character Paul bore was that of an ambassador — from and for Christ, in his room and stead, representing him, sent by him and entrusted with the gospel and a commission to preach it, furnished with qualifications for it (knowledge, faithfulness, wisdom, courage, greatness of mind); Paul describing himself as an ambassador in bonds, that he might speak boldly as he ought — the strange dignity of an ambassador in chains (Paul naming his high office, ambassador of Christ, the King's representative bearing terms of peace, joined to the strange indignity of his condition, in bonds, a chained envoy contrary to all the honour due an ambassador, and praying that even so he may have grace to speak with the boldness his great commission demands, as becomes one who bears the message of the King of heaven).

themes: for which I am an ambassador in bonds
that therein I may speak boldly, as I ought to speak
the strange dignity of an ambassador in chains
the King's envoy bearing terms of peace

schools.summary: Gill: Paul would have them know his affairs, both temporal and spiritual — that he was in bonds, how he was supported under them and of what use they were to others, how he preached in his own hired house and with what success, what ministering brethren he had with him, and in what condition was the church at Rome; Paul declaring that Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord, would make known to them all his affairs and how he fared — the trusted messenger sent with tidings (Paul, knowing the Ephesians' loving concern for him, sending Tychicus, whom he commends as a beloved brother and faithful minister, to bring them word of all his circumstances and welfare, that they might be relieved of anxiety and informed how things stood with the imprisoned apostle).

themes: that ye also may know my affairs, and how I do
Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord
shall make known to you all things
the trusted messenger sent with tidings

schools.summary: Gill: this shows the great concern the apostle had for the churches, being willing to inform them of everything that might be for their use and service; Barnes: the churches where Paul had preached would feel a great interest in his welfare, he being a prisoner at Rome with a doubtful outcome; Paul stating that he sent Tychicus for this very purpose — that they might know his affairs and that he might comfort their hearts — the messenger sent to inform and to comfort (Paul naming the twofold aim: not only that the Ephesians might be informed of his circumstances, but that Tychicus, by his presence and report, might comfort and encourage their hearts, perhaps cast down by anxiety over their imprisoned apostle, so the errand was an act of pastoral love, caring for the comfort of the church even from his prison).

themes: whom I have sent unto you for the same purpose
that ye might know our affairs
that he might comfort your hearts
the messenger sent to inform and to comfort

schools.summary: Gill: 'peace' means all prosperity outward and inward, temporal, spiritual, and eternal — especially peace of conscience under the sprinklings of the blood of Christ and a view of peace made with God by that blood; Paul closing with a benediction of peace to the brethren, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — the apostolic blessing of peace, love, and faith (Paul, drawing toward the end, invoking on the brethren the great gospel blessings: 'peace,' reconciliation with God and the calm of conscience that flows from it, and 'love with faith,' a love joined to and springing from faith, and naming their source, God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together, the one fountain of all grace).

themes: peace be to the brethren, and love with faith
from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ
the benediction of peace, love, and faith
the great gospel blessings invoked

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is the object of love, and a lovely object — to be loved because of the loveliness of his person and the transcendent excellencies in him, his suitableness and fulness as a Saviour, his great love shown to his church and people, and the relations he stands in to them and the communion they have with him; Paul ending the epistle with a benediction of grace on all who love the Lord Jesus Christ in sincerity, sealed with 'Amen' — the closing grace on all sincere lovers of Christ (Paul, with his last words, invoking the grace of God on a marked company: all who love the Lord Jesus Christ 'in sincerity,' with a true, incorruptible, unfeigned love, not in word or pretence only, a love that is itself the mark of the true believer, so the apostle's parting blessing rests on every soul, of whatever place or rank, that truly and sincerely loves the Saviour).

themes: grace be with all them that love our Lord Jesus Christ
in sincerity
the closing grace on all sincere lovers of Christ
sincere love to Christ the mark of the believer
